passage:
A student is writing an essay arguing that wealthy nations bear a disproportionate moral responsibility for climate change mitigation. The student has gathered the following information:

• Historically, the United States, the European Union, and other industrialized nations are responsible for roughly 70% of cumulative global CO₂ emissions since the Industrial Revolution.
• A 2021 Oxfam study found that the wealthiest 1% of the global population produces more than twice the carbon emissions of the bottom 50%.
• Many developing nations have argued in international climate negotiations that binding emissions limits would constrain their economic development and perpetuate existing global inequalities.
question: Which sentence best uses the information above to advance the student's argument?

choices.A: Wealthy nations have the technical and financial capacity to develop and deploy clean energy technologies, which developing nations currently lack.
choices.B: Because industrialized nations have contributed the vast majority of historical emissions and their wealthiest citizens produce far more emissions per capita, they should bear primary responsibility for reducing global emissions.
choices.C: Developing nations are right to resist binding emissions limits, since such limits would prevent them from achieving the economic growth that wealthy nations have already enjoyed.
choices.D: International climate negotiations have repeatedly failed to produce enforceable agreements, suggesting that voluntary commitments are the most realistic path forward.

explanation: The student's argument is that wealthy nations bear disproportionate moral responsibility for climate mitigation. B draws directly on both the first bullet (historical emissions data—70% from industrialized nations) and the second bullet (high per capita emissions of the wealthy) to make a coherent argument for disproportionate responsibility. A introduces a capacity argument not found in the notes. C adopts the position of developing nations from the third bullet, which contradicts the student's argument. D shifts to negotiation effectiveness, which is not the student's thesis.
